g.data-narration: Welcome to Basic Learning Theory - the mathematical foundation of machine learning.
Computational learning theory studies the theoretical foundations - what can be learned, how much data is needed, and what guarantees we have.
g.text: The branch of ML that studies theoretical foundations of learning algorithms.
Answers: What can be learned? How much data? How complex can H be?
Provides mathematical frameworks characterizing when learning is possible

g.data-narration: Learning theory provides guarantees about algorithm performance, guides data collection, and helps select the right approach.
g.text: • Guarantees: Mathematical proof that algorithms will work
• Data Planning: Sample complexity tells how much data needed
• Algorithm Selection: No Free Lunch guides us to try multiple approaches

g.data-narration: Hypothesis Space H is all possible functions an algorithm can consider - like all linear boundaries for linear classifiers.
g.text: Set of ALL possible hypotheses (functions) a learning algorithm can consider
• Determined BEFORE seeing data based on algorithm's design
• Larger H = more expressive but harder to search

g.data-narration: Version Space is the subset of H consistent with training data - it shrinks as more examples arrive.
g.text: Subset of H consistent with ALL training examples: VS = {h ∈ H | h(xᵢ) = yᵢ ∀ training data}
Bounded by: most general + most specific
Empty VS = target not in H

g.data-narration: Sample complexity tells how many training examples we need - more data required for higher accuracy and confidence.
g.text: Minimum training examples needed to learn reliably with guarantees

g.data-narration: PAC Learning formalizes when a concept can be learned - with high probability, we get a hypothesis with low error.
g.text: Framework by Leslie Valiant (1984): guarantees about learning quality
Efficiently PAC-learnable: sample complexity polynomial in 1/ε and 1/δ

g.data-narration: VC Dimension measures expressive power - it is the largest set of points that the hypothesis class can perfectly classify.
g.text: VC(H) = largest number of points that H can SHATTER (perfectly classify all 2ⁿ labelings)
Example: Linear classifiers in 2D can shatter 3 points (not 4)

g.data-narration: No Free Lunch Theorem states no single algorithm is universally best - what works on one problem may fail on another.
g.text: No single learning algorithm is universally best for ALL problems.
Averaged over all possible target functions, every algorithm performs equally.

g.data-narration: Occam's Razor says prefer the simplest hypothesis consistent with data - simpler models generalize better.
g.text: Prefer the simplest hypothesis that is CONSISTENT with the data
Simpler models: less likely to overfit, better generalize, easier to interpret

g.data-narration: Inductive Bias is assumptions a learner makes to generalize - without it, no generalization is possible.
g.text: Assumptions a learning algorithm makes to generalize from training to unseen data
Affects how hypothesis space is explored

g.data-narration: Here are the key formulas to remember - sample complexity, PAC learning, and VC dimension bounds.
Remember these exam tips - PAC with epsilon and delta, VC dimension equals 3 for linear classifiers in 2D.
g.text: 1. Define PAC clearly: P(error ≤ ε) ≥ 1 - δ with both epsilon and delta
2. VC dimension of linear 2D = 3 (most commonly asked specific value)
3. Explain shattering with diagram showing all 2ⁿ labelings
4. H = all possible, VS = consistent with data
6. Link inductive bias to specific algorithms
